chunk_summary: Guidelines for first-hour stabilization in complicated SAM focus on shock criteria, hypoglycemia management, and empiric antibiotics, emphasizing precise protocols to prevent risks during initial care and transfer.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for diagnosing shock in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn the appropriate first-hour management steps for hypoglycemia and hypothermia in complicated SAM.
Identify empiric antibiotic regimens for sepsis in SAM and when to escalate treatment.
Recognize the importance of monitoring for fluid overload during IV therapy in SAM.
key_facts: Shock in SAM is defined as lethargy or unconsciousness with cold hands plus capillary refill >3 seconds or weak, fast pulse.
Administer 5 ml/kg of sterile 10% glucose IV for hypoglycemia if the child is lethargic, unconscious, or convulsing.
Empiric antibiotics: ampicillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily; escalate to cefotaxime or ceftriaxone if no improvement in 48 hours.
Avoid large IV fluid boluses based on a single dehydration sign; monitor for respiratory distress or fluid overload during infusion.
Treat hypoglycemia and hypothermia together with warming and early feeding.
reference_quote: "Indian facility guidance defines shock as lethargic or unconscious with cold hands plus either capillary refill >3 seconds or a weak and fast pulse." (Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Fluid therapy and monitoring in SAM
